steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.sequor, sequi, secutus sum: These are the parts of 'sequi' (to follow) — not 'esse'
hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — not 'esse'
hints.scio, scire, scivi, scitum: These are the parts of 'scire' (to know) — not 'esse'

hints.Genitive singular: 'amicitiae' can be genitive singular, but here it is the subject of 'sunt' (nominative plural)
hints.Dative singular: 'amicitiae' can be dative singular, but context shows it is the nominative plural subject
hints.Vocative plural: While identical in form, Cicero is not addressing friendships — this is the subject

hints.2nd: 2nd declension nominative plural ends in -i — 'amicitia, amicitiae' is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nominative plural ends in -es — 'amicitiae' is 1st declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nominative plural ends in -es — 'amicitiae' is 1st declension

choices: an implied noun not in the sentence

hints.sunt — truly are: 'Verae' is an adjective, not an adverb — it modifies a noun, not a verb
hints.an implied noun not in the sentence: The noun is explicit: 'amicitiae' — 'verae' agrees with it in gender, number, and case

steps.prompt: What philosophical claim does Cicero make here?
choices: Friendship is more important than truth

hints.All friendships are temporary: Cicero says the opposite — 'sempiternae' means everlasting
hints.Friendship is more important than truth: 'Verae' modifies 'amicitiae' — Cicero says TRUE friendships are eternal, not that friendship outranks truth
hints.Only old friendships are real: 'Sempiternae' means everlasting, not old — Cicero speaks of the enduring nature of true friendship
